FairyWinx's blog

Codeforces Round #818 (Div. 2) Editorial.
By FairyWinx, history, 4 years ago, translation,

Problem A. Idea by Igorbunov

Hint 1

Only the following pairs of numbers are possible: (x,x)(x,x), (x,2⋅x)(x,2⋅x), and (x,3⋅x)(x,3⋅x)

Solution

Notice that only the following pairs of numbers are possible: (x,x)(x,x), (x,2⋅x)(x,2⋅x), and (x,3⋅x)(x,3⋅x).

Proof:

Let d=gcd(a,b)d=gcd(a,b). Now notice that it's impossible that a=k⋅da=k⋅d for some k>4k>4, because otherwise lcmlcm will be at least k⋅d>3⋅dk⋅d>3⋅d. Therefore the only possible cases are the pairs listed above and (2⋅d,3⋅d)(2⋅d,3⋅d), but in the latter case we have lcm=6⋅dlcm=6⋅d.

The number of the pairs of the first kind is nn, of the second kind is 2⋅⌊n2⌋2⋅⌊n2⌋, and of the third kind is 2⋅⌊n3⌋2⋅⌊n3⌋ (the factor 22 in the latter two formulae arises from the fact that pairs are ordered). Therefore, the answer to the problem is n+2⋅(⌊n2⌋+⌊n3⌋)n+2⋅(⌊n2⌋+⌊n3⌋).

Problem B. Idea by FairyWinx

Hint 1

Solve the problem for k=2k=2.

Hint 2

Solve the problem without the "(r,c)(r,c) must be colored" limitation.

Hint 3

Try to paint diagonals

Hint 4

No, you don't need casework

Solution

Notice that the answer to the problem is at least n2kn2k, because you can split the square into so many non-intersecting rectangles of dimensions 1×k1×k. So let's try to paint exactly so many cells and see if maybe it's always possible.

For simplicity, let's first solve the problem without necessarily painting (r,c)(r,c). In this case, we're looking for something like a chess coloring, which is a diagonal coloring.

Let's number the diagonals from the "lowest" to the "highest". Notice that every 1×k1×k and k×1k×1 subrectangle intersects exactly kk consecutive diagonals, so we can paint every kk-th diagonal to obtain the required answer: every such subrectangle will contain exactly one painted cell.

To add the (r,c)(r,c) requirement back, notice that (r,c)(r,c) lies on the diagonal number r+cr+c. (Because if you trace any path from (0,0)(0,0) to (r,c)(r,c) with non-decreasing coordinates, going one cell upwards or rightwards increases exactly one of the coordinates by one, and also increases the number of the diagonal by one). Therefore, all we need to do is paint the cells whose coordinates satisfy (x+y)%k=(r+c)%k(x+y)%k=(r+c)%k

Problem C. Idea by FairyWinx

Hint 1

We've got a problem if bi>bi+1+1bi>bi+1+1

Hint 2

It's alright if ai=biai=bi

Hint 3

Programmers aren't mathematicians, you don't need to prove the solution

Solution

Firstly, we obviously require ai≤biai≤bi to hold for all ii. With that out of our way, let's consider non-trivial cases. Also let an+1=a1,bn+1=b1an+1=a1,bn+1=b1 cyclically.

For each ii, we require that either ai=biai=bi or bi≤bi+1+1bi≤bi+1+1 holds. That's because if we increment aiai at least once, we had ai=bi−1ai=bi−1 and ai+1≤bi+1ai+1≤bi+1 before the last increment of aiai, and from here it's just a matter of simple algebraic transformations.

Now let's prove these two conditions are enough. Let ii be the index of the minimal element of aa such that ai<biai<bi (i.e. the smallest element that's not ready yet). Notice that in this case we can, in fact, assign ai:=ai+1ai:=ai+1, because ai≤bi≤bi+1+1ai≤bi≤bi+1+1 holds, and now we're one step closer to the required array. It's easy to continue this proof by induction.

Problem D. Idea by FairyWinx

Hint 1

Reformulate this problem in terms of a complete binary tree.

Hint 2

It would be strange for the sponsors to changes two nodes of the same depth.

Solution

The problem can be reformulated as follows. We've got a complete binary tree with 2n2n leaves. There's a marked edge from each intermediate node to one of its children. The winner is the leaf reachable from the root via marked edges. Changes modify the outgoing marked edge of a node.

Now it should be fairly obvious that there's no reason to change more than one node per level, because only one node matters per level--the one on the path from the root to the answer node. So, the winner only depends on the subset of levels we perform changes on, and vice versa: different subsets always yield different winners.

Sponsors can change exactly ii nodes in (ni)(ni) ways. Summing this over ii, we get ∑min(n,k)i=0(ni)∑i=0min(n,k)(ni). Call this number mm. mm is the number of winners the sponsors choose between--let's call them candidates for brevity. It's easy to see that mm is the answer to the problem, because a) sponsors can guarantee the winner is at least mm, as, independent of the list of candidate winners "provided" by Madoka, at least one of them must be at least mm, and b) Madoka can guarantee the winner is at most mm by firstly marking edges arbitrarily, then computing the list of candidate nodes, and only then fill them with numbers from 11 to mm (and the other nodes arbitrarily).

Problem E. Idea by FairyWinx

Hint 1

Bruteforce cc

Hint 2

gcd(a,b)gcd(a,b) divides a+ba+b.

Hint 3

Recall the existence of the Euler's totient function

Solution

Let's bruteforce cc, then we have gcd(a,b)=gcd(a,a+b)=gcd(a,n−c)gcd(a,b)=gcd(a,a+b)=gcd(a,n−c). This means that gcd(a,b)gcd(a,b) divides n−cn−c, so let's just go through all divisors of n−cn−c. For every factor dd, the count of pairs (a,b)(a,b) satisfying a+b=n−ca+b=n−c and gcd(a,b)=dgcd(a,b)=d is ϕ(n−cd)ϕ(n−cd), because we need dd to divide aa and be coprime with n−cdn−cd, so that the gcdgcd is equal to dd.

Therefore, the answer to the problem is ∑lcm(c,d)∗ϕn−cd∑lcm(c,d)∗ϕn−cd, where 1≤c≤n−21≤c≤n−2 and dd is a factor of n−cn−c.

Problem F. Idea by TeaTime, tutorial by purplesyringa

Editorialist's note: I didn't submit the solution myself, but I proved it theoretically, aggregated solutions of problemsetters as well as participants, so I'm fairly sure it's correct, but you might want to treat it with more suspicion.

Hint 1

Try graph theory.

Hint 2

Try flow theory.

Hint 3

No, that won't work, try again.

Solution

Let's reformulate the problem in terms of graphs. We are given an undirected graph and we are asked to determine edge directions, subject to fixed indegree minus outdegree (hereinafter balance) values for some vertices.

It is tempting to think of this as a flow problem: edges indicate pipes with capacity of 1, vertices are producers or consumers of flow, and vertices with fixed differencies produce or consume an exact amount of flow. Except that's not quite an equivalent problem: a maxflow algorithm will find a flow, i.e. an orientation of edges, but it might just ignore some edges if it feels like it.

We need to overcome this somehow by introducing incentive to use all edges. To do this, forget about the "edges are edges, vertices are vertices" idea for a while. Create an imaginary source and add a pipe with capacity 1 to every edge of the original graph. Technically, this is interpreting edges of the original graph as vertices of the flow graph. Non-technically, I like to interpret this like magically spawning flow into the middle of the edge.

Now, the flow appearing in the edge has to actually go somewhere if we want the maxflow algorithm to treat it like a treasure it wants to increase. Let's just add two pipes: from the edge to vertex uiui and from the edge to vertex vivi, because where else would it go? (technically, any capacity works, but let's use 1 for simplicity) This has a nice side effect of determining the orientation of the edge: if the flow from the edge goes into uiui, it's as if it was oriented from vivi to uiui, and vice versa.

A small problem is that this changes the semantics of the edge orientation somewhat. In the original problem, ui→viui→vi incremented vivi and decremented uiui. In the new formulation, only vivi is incremented, so we need to transform the requirements avav on balances into requirements bvbv on indegrees: bv=av+degv2bv=av+degv2 (and we need to check that the numerator is even and non-negative, otherwise the answer is NO).

How do we enforce the indegree requirements? For all vertices with sv=1sv=1, add a pipe from the vertex vv to an imaginary sink with capacity bvbv. We expect all these pipes to be satiated.

What about vertices with sv=0sv=0? Unfortunately, we can't just add a pipe from the vertex vv to an imaginary sink with capacity ∞∞, because if you have an edge with sv=0sv=0 and su=1su=1, the maxflow algorithm doesn't have any incentive to let the flow go to uu instead of vv, so the pipe from uu to the sink might not get satiated and we might erroneously report a negative answer.

How do we require certain pipes to be satiated? We could theoretically use a push-relabel algorithm, but in this case we can use something much simpler. The sum of capacities of all pipes from the imaginary source is mm. We expect them all to be satiated, so this is the total flow we're expecting. The sum of capacities of all pipes to the imaginary sink we want to satiate is ∑vbv∑vbv. Therefore, there's a surplus of flow of exactly Δ=m−∑vbvΔ=m−∑vbv (if it's negative, the answer is NO). So: create an intermediate vertex, add a pipe of capacity ∞∞ from each vertex with sv=0sv=0 to this intermediate vertex, and a pipe from this intermediate vertex to the imaginary sink with capacity ΔΔ. This will stop the algorithm from relying too much on non-fixed vertices.

Run a maxflow algorithm. Check that every pipe from the source to an edge and every pipe from a vertex to the sink is satiated, or, alternatively, the maxflow is exactly mm. If this does not hold, the answer is NO. If this holds, the answer is YES and the edge orientation can be restored by checking which of the two pipes from an edge is satiated.

Is this fast? That depends on the algorithm heavily.

Firstly, you can use the Edmonds-Karp algorithm. It works in O(FM)O(FM), where FF is the maxflow and MM is the number of pipes. The former is mm and the latter is n+mn+m, so we've got O((n+m)m)O((n+m)m), which is just fine.

Secondly, you can use Dinic's algorithm, which is typically considered an improved version of Edmonds-Karp's, but is worse in some cases. It improves the number of rounds from FF to O(N)O(N), where NN is the number of vertices in the network, which doesn't help in this particular problem, sacrificing the complexity of a single phase, increasing it to O(NM)O(NM), which is a disaster waiting to happen.

Lots of people submitted Dinic's instead of Edmonds-Karp's. I don't know why, perhaps they just trained themselves to use Dinic's everywhere and didn't notice the unfunny joke here.

Luckily for them, Dinic's algorithm still works. You might've heard it works in O(MN2/3)O(MN2/3) for unit-capacity networks, where MM is the number of pipes and NN is the number of vertices, which translates to O((n+m)n2/3)O((n+m)n2/3) in our case, which would be good enough if the analysis held.

Our network is not unitary, but it's easy to see how to make it into one. We use non-unit capacities in three cases:

  • When we enforce the indegree, we add a pipe with capacity bvbv from the vertex to the sink. We can replace it with bvbv pipes of capacity 1. As ∑vbv≤m∑vbv≤m, this will not increase the number of pipes by more than mm, so the complexity holds. Due to how Dinic's algorithm works, replacing a pipe with several pipes of the same total capacity does not slow the algorithm down.

  • Similarly, the pipe from the intermediate vertex to the sink has capacity Δ≤mΔ≤m, so we could theoretically replace it with ΔΔ unit pipes and the complexity would hold.

  • Finally, when we handle vertices with sv=0sv=0, we add pipes from such vertices to the intermediate vertex with capacity ∞∞. However, for each such vertex, the maximum used capacity is actually the count kk of edges incident with vv, so the capacity of such pipes could be replaced with kk. This would obviously have the same effect, and would not slow Dinic down: even if it tries to push more than kk through the corresponding backedge, it will quickly rollback, which affects the time taken by a single phase (non-asymptotically), but not the count of phases. As the sum of kk is O(m)O(m), we're fine again.

Tutorial of Codeforces Round 818 (Div. 2)

  • FairyWinx
  • 4 years ago

I have another solution for D.

We will try to count the number of people for whom we can guarantee that they won't win. Let's call it ansans.

Then the answer will be 2n−ans2n−ans

Let's look at stupid solution and try to optimize it:

stupid
int solve(int n, int k) { //returns 'ans'
    if (n < k || n < 0) {
        return 0;
    }
    if (k == 0) {
        return power(2, n); //let's say if k == 0, then we can guarantee than nobody wins.
    } else {
        //we choose one of our children to be the winner (left or right), let it be left
        int ans = solve(n - 1, k); //left child
        ans += solve(n - 1, k - 1); //right child
        return ans;
    }
}

Pretty familiar, right?)

Let's iterate over values of nn, and try to find a number of times, when kk will be equal to zero. Let's call this new nn = mm. Then we will end up with kk = 0 exactly (n−m−1k)(n−m−1k) times! Why? At first I thought that it has to be (n−mk+1)(n−mk+1), but our last move is always fixed! We have to go to solve(n−1,k−1)solve(n−1,k−1), because we are finding the first time, when kk = 0. Logic here is the same as in Pascal's Triangle.

My solution: 170668170

In problem E, why the number of pairs (a,b) that are co-primes and sums to n is phi[n]? I only knew that phi[n] counts the number of integers between 1 and n which are coprime to n.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    lucasxia01
    4 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    lucasxia01
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +39

    If aa is relatively prime to nn, aa must also be relatively prime to n−an−a.

    The proof is simple: assume n−an−a is not relatively prime to aa, then that implies some d>1d>1 divides n−an−a and aa. However, this implies that dd also divides n−a+a=nn−a+a=n, so aa is not relatively prime to nn, a contradiction.

    Given this, the pairs are just (a,n−a)(a,n−a), where aa is relatively prime to nn, so there are φ(n)φ(n) of them.

    → Reply

Problem A

  • since
lcm(a,b)=abgcd(a,b)lcm(a,b)=abgcd(a,b)
  • given
lcm(a,b)gcd(a,b)<=3lcm(a,b)gcd(a,b)<=3
  • then
ab(gcd(a,b))2≤3ab(gcd(a,b))2≤3
  • since
1<=a<=b<=n1<=a<=b<=n

gcd(a,b) will be in range [1, n], therefore if gcd(a,b)==1 then a=1 and b is multiple of 1 , if gcd(a,b)==2 then b should be multiple of 2 , for gcd(a,b)==3 (b=3*a) ...etc.

  • thus
b=mab=ma

where m is an integer.

ab(gcd(a,b))2=a∗a∗m(gcd(a,am))2=a∗a∗ma2=mab(gcd(a,b))2=a∗a∗m(gcd(a,am))2=a∗a∗ma2=m
  • hence
m≤3m≤3
  • b=a*m , where m={1,2,3}
  • now when m=1 therefore b=a , since 1<=a<=n there will be n solutions
    • example : (1,1), (2,2) ,(3,3)....(n,n)
  • for m=2 b=2*a there will be 2* [n/2] solutions
    • example : (1,2) , (2,1), (2,4), (4,2)....([n/2],2*[n/2]),(2*[n/2],[n/2])
  • for m=3 b=3*a there will be 2* [n/3] solutions
    • example : (1,3),(3,1), (2,6),(6,2)....([n/3],3*[n/3]),(3*[n/3],[n/3])
  • so by proof solution is: n+ 2*[n/3]+2*[n/2]
→ Reply

    gcd(a,b) will be in range [1, n], therefore if gcd(a,b)==1 then a=1 and b is multiple of 1 , if gcd(a,b)==2 then b should be multiple of 2 , for gcd(a,b)==3 (b=3*a) ...etc. - thus b = ma.

In D, the answer is:

[xk](1+x)n1−x[xk](1+x)n1−x

which can be computed using FFT.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    electronaota
    4 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    electronaota
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +6

    I realized that is is absolutely unnecessary to use FFT here after reading the editional. I tried to find the generating function for the answer but it turned out to be just the sum of binomial coefficients.

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    Alan_Zhao
    4 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    Alan_Zhao
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +3

    For all k, [xk](1+x)n[xk](1+x)n is just (nk)(nk). Notice that [xk]F(x)/(1−x)[xk]F(x)/(1−x) is summing up the coefficients of [xj]F(x)[xj]F(x), where j≤kj≤k. So the answer is the sum of binomial coefficients.

    → Reply

can someone please explain the solution to problem A in easy steps?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    beaten_by_ai
    4 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    beaten_by_ai
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +3

    Well, let g=gcd(a,b)g=gcd(a,b) and a=ga0a=ga0 and b=gb0b=gb0, where gcd(a0,b0)=1gcd(a0,b0)=1. So, here we have lcm(a,b)=abgcd(a,b)=g2a0b0g=ga0b0lcm(a,b)=abgcd(a,b)=g2a0b0g=ga0b0. Thus, lcm(a,b)gcd(a,b)=a0b0≤3lcm(a,b)gcd(a,b)=a0b0≤3. So, here we have 33 cases:

    • a0b0=3a0b0=3, in this case we have (a0,b0)=(1,3),(3,1)(a0,b0)=(1,3),(3,1), thus, we have (a,b)=(3k,k),(k,3k)(a,b)=(3k,k),(k,3k). Note that it is sufficient for 3k≤n3k≤n, because this automatically gives k≤nk≤n. Thus, here there are 2⌊n3⌋2⌊n3⌋ solutions. (Because (a,b)(a,b) is not the same as (b,a)(b,a))
    • Similar logic to the above case. Answer is 2⌊n2⌋2⌊n2⌋ in this case.
    • Similar logic to the above case. Answer is nn in this case.

    Adding all of them up, we have the answer as 2⌊n3⌋+2⌊n2⌋+n2⌊n3⌋+2⌊n2⌋+n.

    → Reply
»
electronaota
4 years ago, show #
»
electronaota
4 years ago, hide # |
← Rev. 5 →  
+62

In problem E, the answer is:

ans=∑k=1n∑a=1⌊nk⌋∑b=1⌊nk⌋lcm(n−ak−bk,k)[gcd(a,b)=1][ak+bk≤n]=∑k=1n∑a=1⌊nk⌋∑b=1⌊nk⌋k(n−ak−bk)gcd(k,n)[gcd(a,b)=1][ak+bk≤n]=∑k=1nkgcd(k,n)∑a=1⌊nk⌋∑b=1⌊nk⌋(n−ak−bk)[ak+bk≤n]∑d|gcd(a,b)μ(d)=∑k=1n∑d=1⌊nk⌋kgcd(k,n)μ(d)∑a=1⌊nkd⌋∑b=1⌊nkd⌋−an−akd−bkd=∑k=1n∑d=1⌊nk⌋kgcd(k,n)μ(d)(n⌊nkd⌋(⌊nkd⌋−1)2+kd⌊nkd⌋(1−⌊nkd⌋2)3)=∑k=1n∑d=1⌊nk⌋kgcd(k,n)μ(d)(⌊nkd⌋(1−⌊nkd⌋)(2kd⌊nkd⌋+2kd−3n)6)ans=∑k=1n∑a=1⌊nk⌋∑b=1⌊nk⌋lcm(n−ak−bk,k)[gcd(a,b)=1][ak+bk≤n]=∑k=1n∑a=1⌊nk⌋∑b=1⌊nk⌋k(n−ak−bk)gcd(k,n)[gcd(a,b)=1][ak+bk≤n]=∑k=1nkgcd(k,n)∑a=1⌊nk⌋∑b=1⌊nk⌋(n−ak−bk)[ak+bk≤n]∑d|gcd(a,b)μ(d)=∑k=1n∑d=1⌊nk⌋kgcd(k,n)μ(d)∑a=1⌊nkd⌋∑b=1⌊nkd⌋−an−akd−bkd=∑k=1n∑d=1⌊nk⌋kgcd(k,n)μ(d)(n⌊nkd⌋(⌊nkd⌋−1)2+kd⌊nkd⌋(1−⌊nkd⌋2)3)=∑k=1n∑d=1⌊nk⌋kgcd(k,n)μ(d)(⌊nkd⌋(1−⌊nkd⌋)(2kd⌊nkd⌋+2kd−3n)6)

Where μ(n)μ(n) is mobius function. This can be computed in O(NlogN)O(Nlog⁡N)

shadow9236
4 years ago, show #

shadow9236
4 years ago, hide # |
 
+21

Alternate way of looking at D

Consider the path from each leaf to the root. The path will see "winning" and "losing" edges. Now associate an nn-bit binary number to each leaf. This number will have a 11 at position ii if the ithith edge on the path was a winning edge and 00 otherwise. Convince yourself that this number will be different for each leaf. Now for a leaf to win, all 00s in its number must be changed to 11. So Madoka will assign lower valued leaves, binary numbers with less number of 00s. It is easy to see that the answer is the largest number having kk zeroes in its assigned number which is ∑min(n,k)i=0(ni)∑i=0min(n,k)(ni).

→ Reply

4 years ago, show #

+32

Another way to solve E:

∑lcm(c,gcd(a,b))=∑c⋅gcd(a,b)gcd(a,b,c)=∑(n−a−b)⋅gcd(a,b)gcd(a,b,n−a−b)=∑(n−a−b)⋅gcd(a,b)gcd(a,b,n)∑lcm(c,gcd(a,b))=∑c⋅gcd(a,b)gcd(a,b,c)=∑(n−a−b)⋅gcd(a,b)gcd(a,b,n−a−b)=∑(n−a−b)⋅gcd(a,b)gcd(a,b,n)

We can fix gcd(a,b)=dgcd(a,b)=d, and calculate cnt[d] −cnt[d] − number of pairs (a;b)(a;b) that gcd(a,b)gcd(a,b) has dd as divisor.

cnt[d]=∑k=2k⋅d<n(k−1)∗(n−a−b)=∑k=2k⋅d<n(k−1)∗(n−k⋅d)cnt[d]=∑k=2k⋅d<n(k−1)∗(n−a−b)=∑k=2k⋅d<n(k−1)∗(n−k⋅d)

Then we have to make inclusion/exclusion to make cnt[d] −cnt[d] − number of pairs (a;b)(a;b) that gcd(a,b)=dgcd(a,b)=d.

So, the answer will be ∑d=1d≤ncnt[d]⋅dgcd(d,n)∑d=1d≤ncnt[d]⋅dgcd(d,n).

Overall, O(NlogN)O(NlogN).

iphelf

-13

Problem D can be converted as follows:

Given a complete binary tree with 2n2n different leaf nodes, and initially 2n−12n−1 portals that connect each internal node with its left child node.

Madoka starts at the root node. From one internal node, she can move to its left child node instantly (in zero minutes) via portal. But to move to its right child node, she must walk, and it takes one minute.

Now she wants to know the number of different leaf nodes she can possibly reach within kk minutes.

For example, in a tree with 44 leaf nodes and 33 bridges, initially, Madoka can already reach one leaf node (the left most one) without taking time. If k=1k=1, she can possibly reach 33 different leaf nodes (all but the right most one). If k≥2k≥2, then all 44 leaf nodes.

ivan24

ivan24
4 years ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
+14

Lmao, I think problem F can be solved with some cheesy 2-SAT with random walks. Just keep randomly swapping edges and keep track of indegrees of each node. We know each node v that has s[v] == 1 has a certain required in degree and a required out degree.

If for all nodes, your current in degree is at the required in degree, you have a valid solution. If for all nodes, your current in degree is at the required out degree, you have a valid solution by flipping all the edges. This makes it a lot better than regular 2-SAT since you have 2 possible hits instead of just one, I think this is why you can go quite a bit under the O(n^2) requirement for regular 2-SAT.

I was able to get an AC while upsolving while just using 10^7 iterations, (I expected I'd need more). My solution is literally just 100 lines and I was able to code it tipsy.

Useful Link about 2-SAT: https://www.cl.cam.ac.uk/teaching/1920/Probablty/materials/Lecture7.pdf

→ Reply

    This is not the classical 2-SAT algorithm though. The "default" solution to 2-SAT is computing SCC on the graph of implications. What you're doing is much more generic than 2-SAT, it's kind of gradient descent.

    Did anyone try some sort of simulated annealing?

+3

I've got a good solution for D.

https://postimg.cc/94p4yLvq

In the image above: Basically, I'm trying to find out for each position, how many changes it would take to make that position holder win (when every time Madoka choose left one to win.) So the last line shows, how many changes we will have to make to make that node holder win. (0,1,1,2,...)

We can observe now, suppose we have found out for first 2^i positions. So what will the changes needed for the next 2^i positions?
first 2^i: 1,2,3,4,..k...2^i; second 2^i : 1,2,3,4...k...2^i;

Second k would need changes=changes first k needs+1; (It is easy to see that)

Now we can observe a pattern here. Pascal Triangle Pattern; if n=1 (N=2^1): 0 needed by 1, 1 needed by 1; if n=2 (N=2^2) : 0 needed by 1, 1 needed by 2, 2 needed by 1; ....so on so it would look like: 1 1 1 1 2 1 1 3 3 1 ...

so the final thing we wanna do is just find nth row of pascal triangle and do the prefix sum till min(k,n); and this would be our answer. Coz we are just trying to count how many positions can sponsor make win. So say they can make any position amongst the k positions, then answer would be k. coz Madoka would put the first k smallest there.

Excuse for the poor explanation.

    Damn. I heard the complexity from a friend who heard it from a friend who apparently didn't read the original Karzanov's paper but just read someone's analysis on the internet and misinterpreted it.

    Shit happens. Anyway.

    Firstly, the complexity is reachable, albeit on a different type of networks. You can read Alexander V. Karzanov, On finding a maximum flow in a network with special structure and some applications to find out more.

    Secondly, a slightly worse but still subquadratic complexity holds. I updated the tutorial with the proof.

Can someone please explain problem C's solution in some simple language?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    daksh2021
    4 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    daksh2021
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +1

    As far as I have understood... First of all its obv that for all i, ai <= bi. Then it is necessary that for each element it should be either ai = bi or bi <= bi+1 + 1. Now given that these conditions are satisfied, we can always make both arrays equal in the following way. If at each step we pick the min element such that ai != bi, we are sure that bi <= bi+1 + 1. So lets suppose ai <= ai+1 then we can simply increment ai to ai + 1. The other case, ai > ai+1, is never possible because if ai+1 != bi+1 then we would have chosen ai+1 as the smallest element else if ai+1 = bi+1, then bi > ai > bi+1... which means bi > bi+1 + 1 which contradicts our initial condition. Hence, we will always be able to increment the min element until it reaches its target for each element.

    → Reply

can anyone provide the solution explanation of problem B ?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    chromate00
    4 years ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    chromate00
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Let us try to construct the matrix starting from (r,c)(r,c). Assume the matrix is as follows when there is one X on (r,c)(r,c). (This is the fifth example btw)

    ......
......
......
.X....
......
......

    Note that this mark on (r,c)(r,c) cannot affect other rows or columns, we fill a full diagonal with marks. After this step the matrix is as follows.

    ....X.
...X..
..X...
.X....
X.....
......

    Now, let's find some other cells we need to fill. After we fill these cells which have kk horizontal distance relative to the line, we can see that it has kk vertical distance as well.

    ....X.
X--X..
|.X|..
|X.|..
X--X..
......

    And, of course, these marks did not affect other rows or columns, so we need to construct diagonals based on these cells as well.

    .X..X.
X..X..
..X..X
.X..X.
X..X..
..X...

    And after that we need to construct marks based on the new diagonals, and the diagonals based on the marks, and so on.This goes on until the matrix fits the criteria. By inductive proof (same logic as we did above), we can prove that the diagonals we just marked are the one (r,c)(r,c) is on, and the ones whose horizontal/vertical distance to the original diagonal is a multiple of kk. This gives us a formula, r+c≡x+y(modk)r+c≡x+y(modk) (think of the grid as a graph on a plane, really. you'll get my point). Therefore, the proof is finished and you can construct a relatively simple code based on what we just proved.

    → Reply
